coredata.dc:title: SolidPAC is an interactive battery-on-demand energy density estimator for solid-state batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Dixit, Marm
Parejiya, Anand
Essehli, Rachid
Muralidharan, Nitin
Haq, Shomaz Ul
Amin, Ruhul
Belharouak, Ilias
coredata.dc:description: Solid-state batteries hold the promise to be highly impactful next-generation technologies for high-energy and -power-density rechargeable battery applications. It is crucial to identify the metrics that an emerging battery technology should fulfill to achieve parity with conventional Li-ion batteries, primarily in terms of energy density. However, limited approaches exist today to assess and extrapolate the impact of battery designs and choices of cell components on the cell-level energy density of a solid-state battery. Herein, we introduce the Solid-State Battery Performance Analyzer and Calculator (SolidPAC), an interactive experimental toolkit to enable the design of a solid-state battery for user-specified application requirements. The toolkit is flexible enough to assist the battery community in quantifying the impact of materials chemistry and fractions, electrode thicknesses and loadings, and electron flows on cell energy density and costs and in utilizing inverse engineering concepts to correlate the cell energy density output to materials and cell design inputs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SolidPAC design principles
(A) Schematic diagram showing the components of the developed SolidPAC tool.
(B) Flow chart describing the logic and information flow in SolidPAC. The labels shaded in orange are parameters/metrics calculated by the toolkit.
(C) Schematic diagram of the range of length scales at which parameter/design information is required to design battery cells with the required energy density metrics.
(D) Materials inventory for electrodes and solid electrolytes currently included in SolidPAC.
SolidPAC results
(A) Energy density as a function of electrolyte thickness.
(B) Energy density as a function of N/P ratio. For these calculations, Li7P3S11 (LPS) material is used as the solid electrolyte separator as well as the catholyte material.
(C) Gravimetric energy density for solid-state batteries with differing separator materials in conventional and bipolar stacking.

(D) Volumetric energy density for solid-state batteries with differing catholyte materials in conventional and bipolar stacking. For all calculations, the cathode-active material is assumed to be NMC622, with the cathode composition of NMC622:LPS:PVDF:C as 80:10:5:5, Li metal anode with an N/P ratio of 1.2 (unless mentioned otherwise), and electrolyte thickness of 25 μm (unless mentioned otherwise). The cathode areal loading used for calculations was five mAh/cm−2.
Conventional and bipolar stacking
(A) Schematic diagram showing the architecture of battery packs and modules.
(B) Schematic diagram showing differences in conventional and bipolar stacking when cells are stacked in series configuration.

(C) Ion and current flow pathways in conventional and bipolar stacked systems along with a calculation for effective resistance of the current collector, assuming a 1 × 1×10−5 m current-collector sheet
SolidPAC sensitivity analysis
(A) Sensitivity factors for key variables for cell-level gravimetric and volumetric energy densities.
(B) Schematic diagram describing the pathways identified by sensitivity analysis in SolidPAC for enhancing volumetric and gravimetric energy density.
Analysis of experimental datasets with SolidPAC

(A and B) Correlation plots between (A) gravimetric and (B) volumetric energy densities of solid-state battery reports and the areal capacity of the cathode. The marker sizes are scaled to the respective energy densities, while marker colors represent distinct cathode chemistries.

(C) Experimental data correlation between the fraction of active materials in the composite cathode and the resultant areal capacity of the cathode. The marker sizes are scaled with active material fractions, and the colors represent distinct cathode chemistries.

(D) The experimental spread of gravimetric energy density against calculated cathode thickness. The marker sizes are scaled to excess anodes present in the system, and the colors represent distinct cathode chemistries. Each scatter point represents a unique experimental data. 40–54

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Except for the information included here, default values for all other inputs are utilized.

head.ce:title: SolidPAC is an interactive battery-on-demand energy density estimator for solid-state batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state batteries hold the promise to be highly impactful next-generation technologies for high-energy and -power-density rechargeable battery applications. It is crucial to identify the metrics that an emerging battery technology should fulfill to achieve parity with conventional Li-ion batteries, primarily in terms of energy density. However, limited approaches exist today to assess and extrapolate the impact of battery designs and choices of cell components on the cell-level energy density of a solid-state battery. Herein, we introduce the Solid-State Battery Performance Analyzer and Calculator (SolidPAC), an interactive experimental toolkit to enable the design of a solid-state battery for user-specified application requirements. The toolkit is flexible enough to assist the battery community in quantifying the impact of materials chemistry and fractions, electrode thicknesses and loadings, and electron flows on cell energy density and costs and in utilizing inverse engineering concepts to correlate the cell energy density output to materials and cell design inputs.

Dixit et al. report a toolkit that enables the design of solid-state batteries that cater to specific user-specified metrics. The paper details the development approach and implementation of the toolkit and provides example calculations as well as an evaluation of experimental literature data to demonstrate the applicability of the developed toolkit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The toolkit developed is available in Data S1, and the underlying equations are described in Data S2.

ce:section.ce:para:
High energy-dense and safe secondary batteries are required for a wide range of applications from mobile devices to transportation. 1–4 Solid-state batteries (SSBs) are a promising option as next-generation battery technology due to foreseen energy density and safety advantages. 5–8 A pivotal thrust for SSBs pertains to range anxiety and enables the electrification of the transportation sector. 9
,
10 Key technological improvements rely on the integration of high-voltage and -capacity cathodes, solid electrolytes (SEs), and metallic lithium. 11–14 Technoeconomic studies imply that the value proposition of an SSB compared to with conventional Li-ion battery is strongly contingent on how these components are integrated in prototype cells. 8
,
15
,
16 Today, the reported energy densities of SSBs fall within 150–200 Wh kg−1 considering the weights of anodes, SEs, and cathodes only. 5 Factoring the weight associated with current collectors, cell casing, and ancillary operating components in the calculations would significantly reduce the effective cell energy density. Consequently, the energy density metrics reported for SSBs fall quite short of the conventional Li-ion batteries that exceed 250 Wh kg−1 at the cell level. 17 Enabling a SSB technology requires a careful examination of ongoing research and development (R&D) approaches to guide future cell development toward practical applications.

Current research on SSBs is primarily focused on understanding and evaluating cell chemistries. 5 Broadly, two major focus areas are of significance: (1) high ionic conductivity SEs for use as separators/membranes and/or as catholytes with composite cathodes 18–22 and (2) the stabilization of electrode | electrolyte interfaces. 16
,
23–28 It is, however, extremely important to appraise the overall impact of materials selection, electrode balances, and processing and integration practices employed on the cell’s energy density. Therefore, there is a need to develop facile and user-friendly solutions that can help researchers correlate the choice of materials and design strategies to reasonably estimate cell-level energy density. Herein, we report SolidPAC, which is an experimental interactive SSB design toolkit developed at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory. SolidPAC stands for Solid-State Battery Performance Analyzer and Calculator. The current scope of the toolkit is to provide estimations of cell-level energy densities based on chemical composition, component configuration, and processing parameter inputs by users. It also provides an avenue to carry out inverse design to engineer electrode thicknesses and loadings to meet energy-density goals. The authors expect that this toolkit will be useful for audiences ranging from SSB researchers to industry partners as well as for the non-scientific community to assess the implications of design strategies on SSB performances.

SolidPAC is developed with an overarching goal to enable a “Battery-On-Demand”-design toolkit that will provide design guidelines for battery packs, modules, and cells for specific applications. We envision SolidPAC as a toolkit to provide preliminary design criteria for batteries for any user application based on the demands of the user. The present version of SolidPAC made available through this report incorporates design calculations from materials through cells. Further work on incorporating module and pack calculations is underway for future releases of SolidPAC. The range of information used to enable performance predictions by SolidPAC is summarized in Figure 1A. SolidPAC incorporates user inputs for cathode, anode, and SE chemical compositions, fractions and loadings, electrode thicknesses and architecture, electric vehicle (EV) ranges, cell designs, and ancillary battery components. Cell-level capacity is estimated from the user inputs for pack range/energy/power and pack/module/cell configurations (Figure 1B). These calculations are carried forward from the analogous BatPac developed at the Argonne National Laboratory for conventional Li-ion batteries. 29 Combining cell-level capacity with anode and cathode architecture information provided by the user along with material information made available in the inventory are used to estimate the materials required to fabricate the cells. Subsequently, the component dimensions and the cell-level energy density calculations are performed using the input cell design parameters (Figure 1C). The current implementation of SolidPAC is developed on a LabVIEW back end and is released as a standalone application compatible with a Windows platform (Data S1). The detailed installation procedure along with a binary installer file are provided in Note S1.

For all calculations, a Li-metal battery is considered, with the user specifying a negative-to-positive (N/P) ratio that reflects the Li excess in the battery. At an N/P ratio of 1, it is assumed that 100% of Li is deposited from the cathode lithium feedstock upon the completion of the initial charge, and therefore, the thickness of the anode in the pristine cell is 0 μm. That is, an N/P ratio of 1 represents the case of an anode-free configuration. Similarly, an N/P ratio of 1.2 reflects a 20% Li excess that is incorporated in the cell design and calculations. While the calculations discussed here all contain architectures containing Li metal as an anode, the toolkit provides the flexibility to investigate alternative anode materials. Another important note is regarding the calculation of cathode components. Conventional cell-design tools like BatPAC use a power-based approach to determine cathode and anode thicknesses and loadings in mg/cm2 or mAh/cm2. This requires information such as area-specific impedance (ASI) at various states of charge for the battery. ASI values are currently not available for the multitude of cell chemistries that are being investigated in SSBs. Therefore, the calculations for cathode geometrical dimensions are carried out assuming a cathode areal capacity that is provided as an input by users. However, calculations for total cell resistance to assess the impact of cathode | SE and anode | SE interfaces is included in SolidPAC. The total cell resistance is estimated from the physical dimensions of the electrodes and electrolytes along with the electrode | SE interfacial resistances (ohm/cm2) and transport properties (S/cm) input by a user. Note that this capability also enables us to investigate the influence of the introduction of interfacial layers in the SSB architecture. Introducing interlayers at the anode | SE and cathode | SE interface leads to a reduction of interfacial resistances in SSBs. Using these values instead of pristine interfacial resistance values allows us to infer the impact of the interlayer introduction on total cell resistance and power capabilities of the SSB. This provides an initial understanding of the cell-level resistances and the implications for power densities for SSBs. Detailed descriptions of the equations along with a sample calculation spreadsheet are provided in the Supplemental information.

To explore the capability of the toolkit, a few example datasets generated from SolidPAC are discussed next. In these cases, Li7P3S11 (LPS) SE is used as separator as well as a catholyte. The cathode active material is chosen to be LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2 (NMC622), with the cathode composition of NMC622:LPS:PVDF:C as 80:10:5:5, with an N/P ratio of 1.2. The cathode areal loading used for calculations was 5 mAh cm−2. The impact of the separator thickness on the cell-level gravimetric and volumetric energy density with this configuration is visualized in Figure 2A. At a 25 μm thickness of the separator, this configuration yields energy density of ∼400 Wh kg−1 and ∼1,550 Wh L−1. 5 It should be noted that a basic prismatic cell enclosure design was borrowed from BatPAC to calculate the cell-level energy densities. This is close to the proposed energy densities for SSBs designed for EV applications. However, most reports have separator thicknesses in the range of ∼100s of μm, which would result in energy densities in the range of 150–250 Wh kg−1 and 550–950 Wh L−1. 5 As a comparison, the energy density of a conventional lithium nickel-cobalt-aluminum oxide (NCA)-graphite cell is 250 Wh kg−1 and 570 Wh L−1, 17 while that of an NCA/Gr-Si system is close to 400 Wh kg−1 and 700 Wh L−1. Thus, it is crucial to keep the separator thickness below 100 μm to achieve meaningful cell-level energy densities.

Another aspect is the impact of excess lithium in the cell on the energy density. 30 At 20% excess, the cell energy densities are estimated as ∼400 Wh kg−1 and ∼1,550 Wh L−1 as determined above, decreasing to 308 Wh kg−1 and 707 Wh L−1 at the N/P ratio of 10, and further decreasing to 97 Wh kg−1 and 105 Wh L−1 at the N/P ratio of 100. At the areal cathode capacity of 5 mAh cm−2, the excess anode thickness for N/P = 0.2 is roughly 0.5 μm, while at N/P =10, it is 250 μm. Although they are not typically reported in current studies, the thicknesses of Li employed as an anode in the solid-state cells are several 100s of μm. Therefore, keeping a limited amount of excess lithium in the system is a must for achieving high-energy-density SSBs. It should be noted that previous works highlighted the importance of limiting electrolyte thickness and Li excess in SSB research. 31 Additional datasets showcasing the results for NMC-LPS-LPS and NMC-lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO)-LLZO system with two different composite cathode compositions are highlighted in Table 1 . In this case, SolidPAC provided guidelines for coating thicknesses as well as the quantity of materials to achieve the required areal capacity of 3 mAh cm−2. Dry electrode thicknesses of the range of 60–80 μm are required for achieving the required areal capacity with the cathode compositions identified. As discussed earlier, it also provides initial estimates for cell-level energy densities (Table 1). By comparing the results from LPS and LLZO electrolyte systems, it is apparent that the denser SE can be a better option for an improved volumetric energy density, while LPS can be a better one for a higher gravimetric energy density. The SolidPAC tool enables users to independently quantify the cell-level energy densities based on their specific material combinatory compositions.

Another important faculty of SSBs is the potential to use bipolar stacking (Figures 2C, 2D, and 3 ). 32–36 Bipolar stacking of the cells is suitable to yield an increased cell voltage rating and to reduce the inactive elements in the cell (packaging, electrical connections, etc.) and hence to improve cell energy densities. 37–39 However, limited reports of bipolar-stacked SSBs are available in literature, and not much is understood regarding the bipolar stacking of high-energy SSBs right from the material for the bipolar current collector (electrochemically stable at anode and cathode potentials), electrode balancing, cell integration and assembly, and thermal management all the way to packaging. 34 The following discussion is aimed at providing a general understanding regarding negative current collectors, positive and negative electrodes, and separators, and the inferences regarding energy-density calculations are optimistic and speculative in this regard. Within the module, multiple unit cells are connected in series or in parallel through external wiring. Depending on the cell connections within a module, battery architectures are commonly divided into monopolar or bipolar cells. The monopolar design consists of positive and conventional Li-ion batteries, where the negative tab of one cell is connected electronically to the positive tab of the next cell (Figure 3B). This necessitates that the individual monopolar cell be sealed and packaged and subsequently wired to prevent short circuiting and electrolyte leaking. Compared with conventional stacking, bipolar stacking is suitable in an SSB because the SE mitigates the risk of electrolyte leakage and shorting of the cell. Therefore, the cathode and anode can be coated on two sides of the same current collector, eliminating the need for individual packaging and sealing of unit cells. When assessing the ion and current flow pathways in conventional and bipolar stacking configurations, we observe that these flows are concurrent in bipolar stacking, while conventional packing requires a circuitous route for the current flow. This necessitates additional current-collector tabs, wiring, packaging, and thermal management for the conventional configuration. For a single current-collector sheet, the change in resistance due to the effective area for current flow between the series and bipolar configurations is tens of orders of magnitude based on the assumed dimensions of the current collector. Reduced ohmic resistances due to a reduction of external wiring and current-collector tabs and less use of packaging materials lead to improved power and energy densities of bipolar-stacked SSBs. The influence of a stacking configuration on the cell-level energy densities is evaluated for a range of chemistries as shown in Figures 2C and 2D. It should be noted that the exact differences in the configuration between conventional and bipolar stackings are not well established at this point. Specifically, the metrics for cell packaging, sealing, and electrical connections are approximate, and thus, the calculations are based on assumptions that are detailed in the spreadsheet in the Supplemental information. The resulting metrics for the energy density should not be considered as absolute, but the overall trends are in the right ballpark. A small increase in gravimetric energy density is afforded by transitioning from the conventional stacking to a bipolar stacking configuration due to the reduced weight of packaging and sealing. In contrast, a large increase in the volumetric energy density is observed because of the reduction in volume from the absence of the packaging, sealing, and electrical connections. Overall, transitioning to a bipolar series stacking of SSBs is expected to lead to higher energy and power density batteries. Further work on understanding materials (bipolar plates), architectures, and integrations is required in this direction.

A sensitivity analysis is carried out to assess the key parameters that can be tuned to maximize gravimetric and volumetric energy densities (Figure 4 ). The sensitivity factor is defined as the ratio of change in the output variable for a unit change in the input variable. For this study, the sensitivity factor is estimated by perturbing a given variable by +5% and evaluating the change in the resultant cell-level energy density and is given as
(Equation 1)

where s
i is the sensitivity factor for variable i, and ED

initial are the energy densities of perturbed and base case configurations, respectively. If the sensitivity factor is positive, the resulting cell-level energy density increases with an increase in that variable and vice versa. For the gravimetric energy density, the active material fraction is the most sensitive factor, with areal capacity of the cathode and electrolyte thickness as important factors to tune. In contrast, for the volumetric energy density, areal capacity of the cathode is the most sensitive factor, with electrolyte thickness, active material fraction, catholyte fraction, and the N/P ratio also showing high sensitivity. The sensitivity analysis was carried out for a range of different material combinations, and overall trends are consistent with the results shown here. The approaches to achieving high volumetric and gravimetric energy densities are visually depicted in Figure 4B. The sensitivity matrix for a few material combinations is included in Table S2. It should be noted that the sensitivity analysis offers pathways to tailor cell architectures to achieve the required pack performance. For example, for a battery pack manufacturer wanting to increase the gravimetric energy density of the pack by 10% while extending range capacity by 50 miles, the sensitivity analysis can provide a pathway to achieve this by minimally tweaking the cell configurations. To achieve an increase in the 50-mile range with the required energy density (ED) increase, the pack capacity needs to increase by ∼17 Ah with an increase in areal loading by ∼60% (i.e., 3 to 5 mAh cm−2) while keeping the rest of the cell architecture consistent.

The current consensus in the community is that SSBs will likely be manufactured in a multi-layered prismatic cell format. The ideal operating conditions for SSBs would be comparable to conventional Li-ion batteries at ambient temperatures and pressures. However, systems that run at moderately elevated temperatures (around 50°C) and with higher pressures of several MPa can also be commercially viable. The precise architecture of the modules and packs is highly dependent on the materials chemistry of the SSB and the intended application. In any case, an approximation for the levels of balance of cell components in terms of weight can be made from the designs implemented in conventional Li-ion systems. For a typical Li-ion battery system, the balance of cell components at the cell level takes up roughly 25% of the cell mass. Transitioning from cell to modules in a conventional stacking configuration would lead to a roughly 7% increase in mass, in addition to the cell mass, to incorporate the charge-regulator assemblies, heat-dissipation elements, and enclosures. At the pack level, an additional 15%–20% mass is added to the systems for enclosure and hardware, cooling, and pack management. Note that all of these metrics are based on the design of a conventional Li-ion pack as described in BatPAC. If we assume an analogous requirement for the balance of cell components in a solid-state battery pack, around a 25%–30% increase in the mass of inactive components is anticipated upon transitioning to a pack configuration, resulting in an equivalent decrement in the gravimetric energy density. Naively, a material system offering ∼400 Wh kg−1 at the cell level would only deliver ∼300 Wh kg−1 at the pack level according to the system design. As the SSB community grows and the technology advances, it is necessary for the research community to understand the implications of design strategies beyond the cell-level metrics and at the module and pack levels. We anticipate incorporating more detailed calculations for module and pack designs in the next iteration of SolidPAC.

An extensive compilation and analysis of experimental SSB data was carried out using SolidPAC to further establish and evaluate the application of SolidPAC as a useful tool for SSB research (Figure 5 ; Supplemental information). The marker sizes are scaled to the respective energy densities, while marker colors represent distinct cathode chemistries for Figures 5A and 5B. For Figure 5C, the marker sizes are scaled with active material fractions, and the colors represent distinct cathode chemistries. For Figure 5D, the marker sizes are scaled to excess anodes present in the system, and the colors represent distinct cathode chemistries. Experimental parameters including SSB chemistry (cathode material, catholyte, SE, anode), cathode composition, areal loading, and anode thickness were compiled from an extensive literature survey (Supplemental information). When relevant data were unavailable, appropriate values were assumed to be identical to the strategy employed previously for benchmarking the SSB performance. 5

Subsequently, SolidPAC was used to calculate relevant technological parameters like cathode thickness, excess anode content, and the gravimetric and volumetric energy densities (Figure 5, SI). Gravimetric and volumetric energy density metrics increase with the increase in areal capacity of the cathode regardless of the cathode chemistry (Figures 5A and 5B). Most experimental reports at the cell level fall well short of the state-of-the-art performance of conventional Li-ion batteries due to high SE thicknesses and low areal capacities. However, it should be noted that the packaging to cell mass/volume is skewed at single-layer systems. The gravimetric energy density shows a larger deviation in metrics compared with the volumetric energy density. This is expected, as the form factor in which most of the SSBs are currently tested (coin cells, pressure cells, Swagelok cells) are commonly resulting in consistent cell volumes. The spread is also heterogenous with regards to cathode chemistry, with no preferred candidate. Similarly, the correlation between active material fraction and areal capacity (Figure 5C) also showed a heterogenous distribution, indicating a wide range of cathode areal loading (mg cm−2) and thicknesses (μm) at work for a given composite cathode formulation. Finally, the cathode thickness was correlated to the gravimetric energy density of the battery as well as the excess anode content (Figure 5D). The excess anode content is represented within the size of individual marker points in the scatter plot. It is clearly observed that higher energy densities are achieved at low excess anode contents and higher cathode thicknesses. The present compilation of the experimental data and analysis using SolidPAC offer insight into the directions as well as quantifiable metrics and data that can be employed for achieving the desired metrics.

SSBs are a burgeoning technology for next-generation energy storage. Component design and integration need to be tailored to achieve the purported advantages in energy density and power density over conventional Li-ion batteries. Herein, we showcase SolidPAC, an experimental interactive, graphical user interface toolkit that can be used for SSB design. The overarching goal of SolidPAC is to enable a “Battery-on-Demand”-design toolkit, with the current version providing design metrics up to the cell level with associated metrics for energy density. Example datasets generated from SolidPAC are highlighted along with a discussion regarding the sensitivity analysis for the identification of the key tuning parameters to optimize gravimetric and volumetric energy densities. It is expected that the SolidPAC toolkit will be extremely useful for researchers, industries, and the public who are engaged in unveiling the potential of SSBs. We anticipate that access to such a tool will help guide research toward more practical SSBs.

SolidPAC is an evolving toolbox that will be iterated and improved upon over the years as we learn more about SSB technology. We encourage the research community, industry, and readers at large to engage with them via the communication channels provided below to provide feedback and suggest development areas as well as to seek support for the toolkit (see Lead contact details). We are open to integrating additional modeling components within the SolidPAC framework based on sound feedback. SolidPAC is undergoing active development at Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL), and it is tentatively anticipated that updates to the software will be made on a bi-annual frequency. Updates regarding the software would be shared via the ORNL website and through our social media profiles (LinkedIn, Twitter).
All queries should be addressed by Ilias Belharouak (belahrouaki@ornl.gov).
This study did not produce materials.

The first step for battery designs is identifying pack and cell design specifications based on the inputs provided by the user. Battery design and chemistry selections are populated based on user inputs. These inputs are identified in the “Cell Design Parameters” tab of the included toolkit. Subsequently, the pack and cell design specifications are evaluated as detailed here:
1.
Pack parameter calculations
a.
Energy requirement of pack: this value is based on a standard energy consumption of a light hybrid electric vehicle (LHEV) per mile of travel as estimated by Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS) cycle. It is assumed to be 250 Wh/mile.
b.
Number of modules/pack: product of modules per row and rows of module per pack.
c.
Total cells/system: product of cells per module and number of modules per pack
d.
Pack capacity: if pack capacity is specified as the user input, this value is used. Alternatively, the user can also specify pack energy or vehicle range. In this case, the following two equations are used:

b.
Positive electrode capacity: this value is calculated as the product of the nominal capacity of the cathode, mass fraction of the active material in composite cathode, and the composite-cathode density.
c.
Negative electrode capacity: this value is calculated as the product of the nominal capacity of the anode and the anode density (assuming a 100% dense anode).
d.
System energy: this value is calculated by the product of the cell capacity and the cell voltage at 50% state of charge (SOC).
e.
System voltage: it is the product of cell voltage and number of parallel packs in the system.
f.
Cell voltage: this is defined as the open circuit voltage (OCV) for 50% SOC of the cathode material.

Based on these specifications, the component weights, dimensions, and resistances are estimated. Detailed equations for each variable are included in Data S2. It should be noted here that while certain spreadsheet battery design tools exist, they are difficult to read, interpret, and use on day-to-day basis. SolidPAC is developed as a graphical user interface (GUI)-based software to offer a user-friendly approach toward battery design. The basic operation procedure for using SolidPAC is as follows:
1.
When SolidPAC is run, a GUI window should open (Figure S1).
2.
Controls between the various panes is done through the tabs located near the top of the window.
3.
User inputs are required for the first two panes: “Materials Inventory” and “Cell Design Parameters.”
4.
Users can work with existing materials or add their own materials to the list, ensuring that all requested data are provided when adding new materials.
5.
Specifications regarding the battery are to be provided in “Cell Design Parameters.”
6.
When all inputs are entered, the user should click on the “Calculate” button on the lower left corner of the screen.
7.
“Calculate” button turns green, and the values for cell design are tabulated in the “Cell Calculations” and “Summary of Results” panes.
8.
With the “Calculate” button is pressed, users can modify the inputs to populate the updated design parameters.
9.
The “Export Result Summary” to Excel button, when clicked, exports the current design metrics into a delimited file that can be read by Excel. The variables are arranged as described in the Table S1.
